an2.1:1.2: on one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta's Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
an2.1:1.5: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
an2.1:2.3: Penalties in the present life and penalties in lives to come.
an2.1:2.4: “And which are penalties in the present life?
an2.1:2.5: There is the case where a certain person sees a thief, a criminal, who, when captured by a king, they torture in many ways.
an2.1:2.6: They flog him with whips, beat him with canes, beat him with clubs; they cut off his hands, cut off his feet, cut off his hands & feet; they cut off his ears, cut off his nose, cut off his ears & nose; they subject him to the ‘porridge pot,’ the ‘polished-shell shave,’ the ‘Rāhu’s mouth,’ the ‘flaming garland,’ the ‘blazing hand,’ the ‘grass-duty (ascetic),’ the ‘bark-dress (ascetic),’ the ‘burning antelope,’ the ‘meat hooks,’ the ‘coin-gouging,’ the ‘lye pickling,’ the ‘pivot on a stake,’ the ‘rolled-up bed’; they have him splashed with boiling oil, devoured by dogs, impaled alive on stakes; they have his head cut off with a sword.
an2.1:3.1: “The thought occurs to him [the first man],
an2.1:3.2: ‘It’s because of these sorts of evil actions that when a thief, a criminal, is captured by a king, they torture him in many ways.
an2.1:3.3: They flog him with whips … they have his head cut off with a sword.
an2.1:3.4: And if I were to do this sort of evil action and I, too, were captured by a king, they would torture me in many ways.
an2.1:3.5: They would flog me with whips … they would have my head cut off with a sword.’
an2.1:3.6: “So, fearing penalties in the present life, he doesn’t go around plundering the belongings of others.
an2.1:3.7: “These, monks, are called penalties in the present life.
an2.1:4.1: “And which are penalties in lives to come?
an2.1:4.2: “There is the case where a certain man considers,
an2.1:4.3: ‘Bodily misconduct has an evil result in a life to come. Verbal misconduct has an evil result in a life to come. Mental misconduct has an evil result in a life to come.
an2.1:4.4: If I were to engage in bodily misconduct, verbal misconduct, & mental misconduct,
an2.1:4.5: then how could I not—on the break-up of the body, after death—re-appear in a plane of deprivation, a bad destination, a lower realm, in hell?’
an2.1:4.6: “So, fearing penalties in lives to come, he abandons bodily misconduct and develops right bodily conduct. He abandons verbal misconduct and develops right verbal conduct. He abandons mental misconduct and develops right mental conduct. He keeps himself pure.
an2.1:4.7: “These, monks, are called penalties in lives to come.
an2.1:4.10: ‘We will fear penalties in the present life. We will fear penalties in lives to come. We will be wary of penalties, seeing danger in penalties.’
an2.1:4.11: That’s how you should train yourselves.
an2.1:4.12: It is to be expected of one who is wary of penalties, who sees danger in penalties, that he will be released from all penalties.”
an2.2:1.1: “Monks, there are these two exertions that are hard to maintain in the world.
an2.2:1.3: The exertion of laypeople living at home for the sake of producing cloth, almsfood, lodging, & medicinal requisites for curing the sick. And the exertion of those who have gone forth from home for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.
an2.2:1.4: “These are the two exertions that are hard to maintain in the world.
an2.2:2.1: “Of these two exertions, this is the foremost: the exertion for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.
an2.2:2.3: ‘We will exert ourselves in the exertion for the sake of relinquishing all acquisitions.’
an2.2:2.4: That’s how you should train yourselves.”
an2.5:1.1: “Monks, I have known two qualities through experience:
an2.5:1.2: discontent with regard to skillful qualities and unrelenting exertion.
an2.5:1.4: ‘Gladly would I let the flesh & blood in my body dry up, leaving just the skin, tendons, & bones, but if I have not attained what can be reached through manly firmness, manly persistence, manly striving, there will be no relaxing my persistence.’
an2.5:1.5: From this heedfulness of mine was attained awakening. From this heedfulness of mine was attained the unexcelled freedom from bondage.
an2.5:1.6: “You, too, monks, should relentlessly exert yourselves, (thinking,)
an2.5:1.7: ‘Gladly would we let the flesh & blood in our bodies dry up, leaving just the skin, tendons, & bones, but if we have not attained what can be reached through manly firmness, manly persistence, manly striving, there will be no relaxing our persistence.’
an2.5:1.8: You, too, in no long time will enter & remain in the supreme goal of the holy life for which clansmen rightly go forth from home into homelessness, directly knowing & realizing it for yourselves in the here & now.
an2.5:1.11: “Gladly would we let the flesh & blood in our bodies dry up, leaving just the skin, tendons, & bones, but if we have not attained what can be reached through manly firmness, manly persistence, manly striving, there will be no relaxing our persistence.”’
an2.5:1.12: That’s how you should train yourselves.”
an2.9:1.1: “Monks, these two bright qualities guard the world.
an2.9:1.4: If these two bright qualities did not guard the world, there would be no recognition of ‘mother’ here, no recognition of ‘mother’s sister,’ ‘uncle’s wife,’ ‘teacher’s wife,’ or ‘wives of those who deserve respect.’
an2.9:1.5: The world would be immersed in promiscuity, like rams with goats, roosters with pigs, or dogs with jackals.
an2.9:1.6: But because these two bright qualities guard the world, there is recognition of ‘mother,’ ‘mother’s sister,’ ‘uncle’s wife,’ ‘teacher’s wife,’ & ‘wives of those who deserve respect.’”
